summary: Lead_AutoConvert_BadBulk is a record-triggered flow that loops over the triggering Lead collection, runs a Get Records on Account and an Update Records on Lead inside the loop, and provides no fault path on the Update. SOQL-in-loop and DML-in-loop both escalate to P0 because record-triggered flows fire in batches of 200, multiplying the per-element cost into governor-limit territory. Per the automation-selection decision tree, the recommended verdict is FIX_IN_PLACE: the logic is well within Flow's expressive surface; the failure mode is the loop topology, not the platform choice. Migration to Apex is not justified.
confidence_rationale: Flow XML was fully inlined and the loop/SOQL/DML topology is unambiguous; MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the fixture lacks a live org probe to confirm whether downstream automations (PB, triggers) compound the cost.

findings.title: Get Records inside a loop on the triggering Lead collection
findings.detail: Get Records (Account by Lead.Company) sits inside a Loop iterating $Record collection. At a 200-record bulk insert, this fires 200 SOQL queries — 2× the 100-SOQL governor limit before the loop completes.
findings.recommendation: Lift the Get Records OUT of the loop. Build a Collection Variable of all Lead.Company values first, then run a single Get Records WHERE Account.Name IN {!collectionVar}. Use an Assignment + Map-style lookup inside the loop instead of a fresh query.

findings.title: Update Records inside the loop
findings.detail: Update Records on Lead fires once per loop iteration. At 200 records this is 200 DML statements vs the 150 governor cap.
findings.recommendation: Inside the loop, Assign the per-Lead changes to a Collection Variable. After the loop ends, run a single Update Records on the collection. This collapses the work to one DML statement.

findings.title: Update Records has no fault connector
findings.detail: If the Update fails (validation rule, FLS, locking), the flow halts with a generic error. No fault path means no recoverable behavior, no audit row, and no admin notification.
findings.recommendation: Wire a Fault connector from Update Records to a subflow that writes to Application_Log__c (see templates/flow/FaultPath_Template.md). For record-triggered flows specifically, route the fault to a Platform Event so the failure is visible without blocking the trigger.

deliverables.title: Recommended fix-in-place rewrite outline
deliverables.content:
# Lead_AutoConvert_BadBulk — fix-in-place rewrite outline

**Verdict:** FIX_IN_PLACE per `standards/decision-trees/automation-selection.md` (record-triggered, declarative-friendly logic, no callout/heap pressure that would force Apex).

## Topology rewrite
1. **Outside the loop** — `Get Records` Account WHERE Name IN `{!Lead.Company}` collected from `$Record` triggering rows. Output: `accountsByName` map-shaped output.
2. **Loop** over `$Record`. Inside the loop:
   - `Decision`: does `accountsByName` contain `loop.Lead.Company`?
   - `Assignment`: set `loop.Lead.AccountId` from the matched Account.
   - `Assignment`: append the modified Lead to `leadsToUpdate` collection variable.
3. **After the loop** — single `Update Records` on `leadsToUpdate` with a **Fault connector** wired to a `LogFault` subflow (see template).

## Why FIX_IN_PLACE, not MIGRATE_TO_APEX
- Logic is conditional + assignment + DML; no Apex-only feature is in play (no callout, no recursion, no fan-out > 200).
- Maintenance cost in Flow is lower than the equivalent trigger-handler.
- The bulk-safety bug is the loop topology, not the platform.

## What MIGRATE_TO_APEX would buy you
- Easier unit testing (`@IsTest` is more expressive than Flow Tests).
- Better debugger ergonomics.
- If Lead auto-conversion grows to integrate with a queueable callout, revisit.

process_observations.observation: Two P0 governor-limit bugs sit in the same flow path; either alone would brick a 200-record bulk insert and either fix without the other still leaves the flow brittle.

Fixture does not show whether other automations on Lead (Apex triggers, Process Builders) also fire on insert — if any do, the recommended consolidation may need to align with trigger-consolidator's plan.
Once the flow is rewritten, generate a Flow Test covering the bulk path (200 Leads) so regressions show up at design time.
process_observations.followup_reason: because Flow Tests cover the happy path but a paired Apex test on the surrounding service still needs the canonical 200-record bulk pattern.

citations.used_for: Loop-vs-bulk topology, the lift-the-Get-out-of-the-loop pattern, and the after-loop single Update Records idiom.

citations.branch: Record-triggered + declarative-friendly + no callout/recursion → FIX_IN_PLACE in Flow

followups.because: because if other Lead automations exist (Apex triggers / PBs), they should be consolidated into a single canonical handler to avoid order-of-execution conflicts with the rewritten flow.
because the Apex side that downstream services use to consume converted Leads needs a 200-record bulk test to confirm the rewrite did not regress capacity.
